item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article was author-taken, and as such is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: Sorry for not getting back for so long.
item.content:encoded:
The whole article was inspired by the various creative ways you can turn human ash into objects. Whether it be a ring or even a music record (actual thing you can do). It got me thinking 'What if there was an equivilent to that for tea drinkers?' Then I started realized just how seriousely some people would be about their personal hobbies and obsessions.

These teapots are made specificely for people who are not only passionate about tea, but adore it to the point that they would happily ask for their remains to be made into their own unique flavour of tea for their fellow tea enthusiasts to drink.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this, it's short but still manages to make me question what's currently in mug.

My only question is, how the hell was the anomaly discovered? Who in their not-so right mind is boiling human ashes, and what are the odds that they have the one tea kettle that can create a whatever the hell a 3401-1 is?
Am I reading to deep into this? Yes. Yes I am.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first question is best left ambiguous.

As for the third and fourth, yes there would still be a jelly body created and the green tea appears from the ashes of people who have accepted death. Kind of a way to present 'purity' and peace.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have questions about a couple things: How alive are these brews? What would happen if the foundation tried to dissect or psychically analyze the jellies? Also, What happens if you burn a portion of a living person's flesh to produce one teaspoon of ash?

Also, I'm confused as to why the old man's remains didn't produce any jelly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I may be the only person morbid enough to think of this, but… what happens if a portion of a living person is cremated and the ashes are boiled inside the pot?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll admit recognising subtle imagery is not my forte, but the way you tie the flavours into how these people spent their last moments is very, very good. I didn't get the suicide/salt-water or homicide/sweat/perfume connections at first, probably because I was incredibly tired, but I feel confident giving this an upvote upon re-reading.

Nice work, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Short and sweet. I like it.

It would be cool if there were a supplemental test log where others could add their own 'flavors'.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not what I thought at first
but I like it!
Good Job!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now that a test log is present, let me re-assess it.

It's not what I envision, but it did have me thinking about the people who died.

The rest of the story is now present, at the very least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So here's where I'll mention that the object does in fact have a narrative.

It's not your standard story, with identifiable characters facing some threat or what have you, but here's what you do have:

A hypothetical person died. The teapot was used to prepare tea from their ashes. The tea became some semi-liquid mass that might be alive. Someone drinks this tea and notes its flavor.

We are left with questions. Did someone just consume someone else? Was some essence of the person resurrected? What exactly is this tea?

In all cases, we are left with a possible state for events, actions and consequences, all with the minimum for defined characters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I started wondering what the tea would taste like if the person died of, say, multiple organ failure due to an acute illness.

Really starting to wish I hadn't. Take my +1, you monster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to like it. However, it lacks something. Here, you have established the premises. A narrative is not necessary here, but you did not do much with the premises you have established.

Allergic reaction to almonds: slight nutty taste, death by burning: smokey, drowning: sea air and fish, homicide: decaying wood, iron and compost.

Perhaps that is the only development you have made that branched out from the premises. You can do so much more with the list. You can even surprise us with some interesting tidbits. Like a person who is known to have died in a specific way, but the taste experienced via the SCP can be something different - suggesting a greater context to be inferred.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this for the same reason a lot of people will dislike it; it's a series I style magical object without a narrative. A lot of people insist that series IV scps need a narrative, but I think a magic object is still worth an upvote if it's done right.

This teapot is creepily morbid and is sufficiently unique to get an upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short, sweet, and doesn't overstay its welcome. Doesn't wow me, but doesn't need to.
